LAW AND MOTION PETITIONER JOHN DOE'S APPLICATION FOR AN ORDER SEALING ALL PLEADINGS AND EXHIBITS IS GRANTED IN PART AND DENIED IN PART. PETITIONER IS ENTITLED TO AN ORDER SEALING ALL EXHIBITS TO DECLARATIONS CONSTITUTING INFORMATION PROTECTED PER PENAL CODE 832.7. PETITIONER HAS FAILED TO SATISFY HIS BURDEN OF SHOWING HOW HIS OVERRIDING INTEREST IN HIS RIGHT TO PRIVACY AS A PEACE OFFICE WOULD BE PREJUDICED IF INFORMATION OTHER THAN WHAT IS PROTECTED BY SECTION 832.7 IS MADE AVAILABLE TO THE PUBLIC. COUNSEL FOR PETITIONER TE RESUBMIT A PROPOSED ORDER WHICH PRECISELY IDENTIFIES THE INFORMATION THAT IS PROTECTED BY SECTION 832.7 AND THUS TO BE SEALED AND COMPLIES WITH CRC 2.550(D) AND (E) IN COMLIANCE WITH CRC 3.1312. Notice Of Motion And Defendant City And County Of San Franciscos Motion For An Order To Requiring Plaintiff John Doe To Proceed Under True Name
Off Calendar Feb-27-2019 Granted Per Written Order
MINI MINUTES: Appearances:
Harry Stern, Esq. 415-341-9341 of Rains Lucia Stern PC for Plaintiff.
Rafal Ofierski, Deputy City Attorney 415-554-3800 from the Office of the Office of the City Attorney for Defendant CITY AND COUNTY OF SAN FRANCISCO.

RULING - A R G U E D. The Court shares its views and raises 2 problems. Plaintiff argues. Defendant agrees with the tentative ruling.(as fully reported in the reporter's notes)
The Court having reviewed the pleadings and having heard oral argument rules as follows:

DEFENDANT CITY AND COUNTY OF SAN FRANCISCO'S Motion For An Order To Requiring Plaintiff John Doe To Proceed Under True Name.

Defendant City and County of San Francisco's motion for an order requiring Plaintiff "John Doe" to proceed under his true name is granted. While there is no blanket rule against the use of fictitious names by plaintiffs in cases where (as here) such a procedure is not explicitly authorized by statute, a plaintiff may not use a fictitious name except when unusual circumstances justify protecting his true identity, such as when identification creates a risk of retaliatory physical or mental harm, when anonymity is necessary to preserve the plaintiff's privacy in a matter of sensitive and highly personal nature, or when the anonymous party is compelled to admit his intention to engage in illegal conduct, thereby risking criminal prosecution. (Doe v. Lincoln Unified School Dist. (2010) 188 Cal.App.4th 758, 767, citing Does I thru XXIII v. Advanced Textile Corp. (9th Cir. 2000) 214 F.3d 1058, 1067 [dicta]; see, e.g., Doe v. Superior Court (Luster) (2011) 194 Cal.App.4th 750, 754 [rape victim permitted to file complaint under fictitious name in order to preserve her anonymity].) Here, Plaintiff has not shown that the disclosure of his identity would jeopardize his physical safety, subject him to retaliation, or otherwise cause an unwarranted invasion of privacy that would outweigh the public right of access to court records. He contends that his termination was previously the subject of television news stories that appear to have relied on information from his confidential disciplinary personnel files that he believes were illegally leaked to the news outlet, but he has not shown that as a result of such news coverage, he was previously subjected to any retaliation or threats to his physical safety. Further, the prior disclosure of Doe's identity and other personal information undermines his current claim of a compelling privacy interest in such information that would justify him in proceeding anonymously.(as fully reported in the reporter's transcript)

Order is signed. (see scanned order for details)

Judge: Ethan P. Schulman, Clerk: M. Goodman, Court Reporter: Maria Torreano, CSR#8600, maria.torreano@gmail.com, Reported. =(302/EPS)

Notice Of Hearing (On Petition To Confirm Arbitration Award)
Off Calendar Sep-25-2018 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES: Appearances:
Harry Stern, Esq. 415-341-9341 of Rains Lucia Stern PC for Plaintiff SAN FRANCISCO DEPUTY SHERIFFS' ASSOCIATION, JOHN DOE.
Rafael Ofierski, Deputy City Attorney, 415-554-4244 of the Office of the City Attorney for Defendant City and County of San Francisco.

RULING - A R G U E D. Defendant argues. The Court discloses his knowledge of the players; none has any bearing on the Court's ability to be fair and impartial. Plaintiff argues. The Court having reviewed the pleadings and having heard oral argument rules as follows:

PLAINTIFF SAN FRANCISCO DEPUTY SHERIFFS' ASSOCIATION, JOHN DOE'S Hearing On Petition To Confirm Arbitration Award.

The Court adopts the tentative ruling. Petitioners San Francisco Deputy Sheriffs' Association and John Doe's petition to confirm arbitration award is granted. The arbitration award is confirmed. There is no ground to dismiss, vacate or correct the award. The parties entered into a valid agreement to arbitrate John Doe's grievance of his termination of his employment. Per Douglass v. Serenivision, Inc. (2018) 20 Cal.App.5th 376, 387-38, under ordinary contract law parties may agree to arbitrate a matter: (1) "in a contract signed before a dispute arises", (2) "in a binding stipulation to arbitrate entered into after a dispute has arisen"; or (3) they may "enter into an implied in fact agreement to arbitrate through their conduct (which may additionally be deemed to estop them from denying such an agreement)" and whether parties' conduct constitutes such a consent is a fact specific inquiry. The evidence of the parties' conduct prior to and after the arbitration demonstrates either an express or implied agreement for binding arbitration. Doe's request for "binding arbitration" by a "neutral arbitrator" was not objected to by respondents, respondents' counsel requested a list of arbitrators; the parties stipulated at the hearing to the arbitrator's jurisdiction on all question of law and evidence, both Sheriffs Mirkarimi and Hennessey took part in the proceedings as witnesses, and respondents' counsel confirmed that the department was implementing the award. An express or implied agreement constitutes a waiver of respondents' right to exercise discretion whether to accept the decision of a hearing officer under the MOU. Toal v. Tardif (2009) 178 Cal.App.4th 1208 is distinguishable. In Toal, the attorney was retained for litigation, the client stated that he expressed his lack of agreement to arbitrate to his attorney and the court held that the attorney's signature alone was not sufficient evidence that the client consented to arbitration. Unlike Toal, two counsel for respondents expressed their consent to arbitration and the Sheriff and the former Sheriff participated as a witnesses in the arbitration proceeding.

Order is signed. (see scanned order for details)

Judge: Harold Kahn, Clerk: M.Goodman, Court Reporter: Maria Torreano, CSR#8600, torreanom@aol.com, Reported. =(302/HK)
